narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average among students in our dataset, along with an LSAT score of 32, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated your probability of failing at 47.1%—a risk level that unfortunately tipped the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your academic record that carried the most weight. Your undergraduate GPA was the strongest positive factor, actively working against a failure prediction by showcasing solid performance that the model associates with bar passers. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of about 5.5—emerged as the top concern, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar struggle, likely because lower deciles signal challenges in sustaining high performance. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the typical 5.7 average, added further caution, reinforcing doubts about early academic momentum compared to peers who passed the bar. While your LSAT and family background played roles, these decile rankings overshadowed your strengths and set you apart from the majority of students whose profiles predict bar passage. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially with your above-average GPA and full-time commitment showing real dedication. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting those decile rankings through targeted academic strategies or additional coursework that demonstrates upward trends. Consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above the 36.7 average, as even modest gains could shift the balance. Reaching out to advisors for personalized prep on bar-relevant skills might also help reframe your story. With persistence, you can address these gaps and present an even stronger case next time.
